summary: DeVault proposes a new category of operating system: the 'vendor-purpose OS.' He argues that historically, operating systems were either general-purpose (Unix, Linux) or special-purpose (FreeRTOS, Rockbox), defined by the problems they solve for users. However, Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS increasingly make design decisions that benefit the vendor at the expense of the user. These anti-features—ads, spyware, walled gardens, forced accounts, developer rackets—make the OS worse at both general and special purposes while optimizing for vendor profit.
key_points: Operating systems have historically been categorized as general-purpose or special-purpose based on the problems they solve for users
Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS are increasingly optimized for vendor benefit at the user's expense, warranting a new category
Windows anti-features include ads, spyware, forced Microsoft accounts, and aggressive browser switching pressure
Apple builds walled gardens, enforces developer rackets via cryptographic signing, and designs hardware anti-features to extract profit
Vendor-purpose changes make these OSes worse at both general-purpose and special-purpose tasks
money_quotes: A disturbing trend is emerging in which the user is not the party whose problems are being solved, and perhaps this calls for a new term.
Increasingly, these operating systems are making design decisions which benefit the vendor at the expense of the user.
They use cryptographic signatures to enforce a racket against developers who just want to ship their programs.

They've also long been making similar moves in their hardware design, adding anti-features which are explicitly designed to increase their profit — adding false costs which are ultimately passed onto the consumer.
The operating system is becoming less suited to its general-purpose tasks, as the vendor-purpose anti-features deliberately get in the way.
I am going to start refering to these operating systems as 'vendor purpose', generally alongside a curse and a raising of the middle finger.
key_insight: Major commercial operating systems have shifted from serving users to serving vendors, creating a new category—vendor-purpose OS—where anti-features that extract profit actively degrade the user experience.
